name: mediterranean dal
instructions:
The chunky-everything lentil dal — the most 'curry' of the family. Practical daily variant because lentils go into the pot dry alongside the masala; no separate pre-cook required.

SHARED SPICE BASE: cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, kashmiri chili powder, cardamom, plus kasoori methi and cilantro at the finish.

DISTINCTIVES:
- MEDITERRANEAN BROWN LENTILS as the legume (or french green lentils — similar structural behavior). Masoor dal / red lentils belong in coconut-based dishes instead (see coconut mung bean curry family).
- ONIONS: CHUNKY of any color — red, yellow, or white all fine. Coarsely chopped, generously. This is the variant where you actually SEE onion pieces in the finished dish and that's the point.
- WHOLE SPICES bloomed in oil: bay leaf, cardamom.
- LOTS OF CHUNKY PEPPERS — anaheim, serrano, jalapeño, and even bell pepper. This variant reads closer to a homestyle indian curry with visible chunky vegetables than to the smoother stew-like registers of chole/chana masala/rajma.
- FRESH TOMATOES, chopped coarse. Tolerates some tomato paste alongside for depth.
- Ghee or vegetable oil both fine.
- FINISH: BOTH cilantro and kasoori methi.

PROCEDURE:
1. Heat ghee or vegetable oil. Bloom bay leaf and cardamom briefly.
2. Add coarsely-chopped onions (any color). Cook until softened but keep them chunky.
3. Add ginger, garlic, and generous chunky peppers (anaheim, serrano, jalapeño, bell — mix and match). Sauté briefly.
4. Add ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, kashmiri chili powder, chili powder). Toast.
5. Add coarsely-chopped fresh tomatoes and optional tomato paste. Cook down.
6. Add DRY brown lentils (rinse first; overnight soak helps but not required) and water/stock to cover generously. Simmer covered until lentils are tender (~30-45 min depending on the lentil).
7. Finish with kasoori methi crumbled in and a generous handful of chopped cilantro.
notes:
This recipe used to be Variant C of a master-tomato-dal file. Now standalone.

WHY 'MEDITERRANEAN DAL': the name reflects the lentil choice — mediterranean brown lentils (or french green lentils) rather than the more traditionally-indian masoor dal (red lentils). The name isn't standard indian terminology; it's shorthand for 'the indian tomato dal that uses mediterranean brown lentils.'

=== THE MOST 'CURRY' OF THE FAMILY ===

LOTS OF PEPPERS AND CHUNKY ONIONS OF ALL KINDS: mediterranean dal's flavor and texture read closer to a homestyle indian curry with visible chunky vegetables than to the smoother stew-like registers of chole/chana masala/rajma. Lean INTO the peppers — poblano, serrano, jalapeño, anaheim, and even bell pepper all belong, and generously. Chunky onions of all colors (red, yellow, white) are fine and expected. This is the variant where you actually see and taste discrete pepper and onion pieces in every spoonful, and that visibility is the point.

=== GREEN PEPPER AFFINITY — SHARED WITH CHOLE, BUT TOLERATES MILDER ONES BETTER ===

Mediterranean dal and chole-masala are the two family variants with strong affinity for green peppers. Chole leans toward the spicier ones (poblano, jalapeño, serrano) and doesn't do well with the milder ones. Mediterranean dal takes both — the spicy varieties AND the milder anaheim and bell peppers — because the chunky-curry format has room for pepper variety, and the anaheim/bell peppers add vegetal-sweet bulk that helps carry the chunky-everything identity. Reach for spicy peppers when you want more heat, milder ones when you want more visible vegetable, or mix. Chana masala and rajma DON'T take green peppers well — see those recipes for context.

=== LENTIL CHOICE ===

MEDITERRANEAN BROWN LENTILS PREFERRED, RED LENTILS BELONG IN COCONUT DISHES: mediterranean brown lentils (or french green lentils, similar structural behavior) are what this recipe is tuned for. They hold their shape through the cook and provide the structural bite that keeps the dish from turning into total paste. Masoor dal (red/orange lentils) works technically but disintegrates faster, and — more importantly — red lentils are better used in coconut-based dishes (see coconut mung bean curry family) where the coconut milk carries their softer texture. Save red lentils for the coconut path; use mediterranean brown lentils here.

=== CHUNK IS THE POINT — DON'T PUREE ===

The whole texture logic of this recipe rests on coarse onions and chopped tomatoes providing bite against the disintegrating lentils. If you puree the base (like chole or rajma), the entire dish reads as uniform paste with no textural interest. This is the recipe where chunky is a feature, not a compromise.

=== PRACTICAL ADVANTAGE — DRY LENTILS INTO THE POT ===

This is the everyday variant because the lentils don't need a separate pre-cook. Soaking overnight helps (softer texture, faster cook) but isn't strictly required. You save the pressure-cook step that chole and rajma require. This is why mediterranean dal ends up in weeknight rotation while chole/rajma are weekend projects.

=== TOMATO PASTE IS TOLERATED (UNLIKE CHOLE) ===

Unlike chole (which wants fresh tomatoes only), mediterranean dal tolerates a small addition of tomato paste alongside the fresh tomatoes for depth. Rajma is the paste-only variant; mediterranean dal is the fresh-plus-optional-paste middle path.

=== SHARED FAMILY RULES ===

KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER: color and gentle heat without overpowering. Different from generic chili powder. Non-optional across the tomato-dal family (chole, chana masala, mediterranean dal, rajma).

KASOORI METHI: non-optional in this family. Load-bearing flavor, not a garnish.

SIBLING RECIPES:
- chole-masala: the darker, thicker, more aromatic chickpea preparation with black tea + cardamom + cloves + cinnamon.
- chana-masala: the lighter, tangier, lemon-and-peppers-forward chickpea preparation.
- rajma: the kidney bean gravy — tomato paste concentrated, ghee non-optional, methi-forward.
